items.question: Who uses cross-validation in a typical ML project?
items.answer: Data scientists and ML engineers during model development compare algorithms and hyperparameters using CV scores before final test-set sign-off; statisticians use the same resampling logic for error estimation on limited samples.

items.question: What is k-fold cross-validation?
items.answer: Data are split into k disjoint folds; for each fold i the model trains on the other k−1 folds and is scored on fold i. The CV score is usually the mean of the k validation metrics, with std across folds indicating stability.

items.question: When should you prefer stratified k-fold over plain k-fold?
items.answer: Use stratified k-fold for classification (especially imbalanced classes) so every fold preserves global class proportions and metrics like recall, F1, and PR-AUC remain defined and comparable across folds.

items.question: Where does cross-validation run relative to a locked test set?
items.answer: CV typically runs on the train and validation portion (or full development set excluding the test set) to compare models; the locked test set is evaluated once after CV-based selection, mirroring the train/val/test workflow.

items.question: Why report std of fold scores in addition to the mean?
items.answer: High spread across folds signals unstable generalization—often high variance in the learning process—whereas a good mean with low std suggests the model performs consistently across partitions.

items.question: How do you run leakage-safe k-fold CV in scikit-learn?
items.answer: Wrap preprocessing and the estimator in a Pipeline, pass a KFold or StratifiedKFold splitter to cross_val_score or cross_validate, and never fit scalers or selectors on the full X before splitting.

items.question: How does k-fold CV compare to a single hold-out validation split?
items.answer: A single hold-out uses one partition and can be noisy when n is small; k-fold averages k different partitions, using nearly all data for both training and validation across rounds. Hold-out is cheaper; k-fold is more stable for comparison during development.

items.question: How does stratified k-fold differ from plain k-fold for classification?
items.answer: Plain k-fold assigns rows randomly to folds, which may omit rare classes from a validation fold. Stratified k-fold assigns points so each fold mirrors global class proportions, making classification metrics reliable on imbalanced data.

items.question: What if you scale features on the full dataset before cross_val_score?
items.answer: Validation folds see global mean and variance computed from their own rows, leaking information into training and inflating CV scores. Fix by using a Pipeline so StandardScaler fits only on each fold's training indices.
